literal: the chosen / desired deity; from iṣṭa ("chosen, desired") + devatā ("deity")
invariant_definition: Chosen-deity practice — the cross-tradition Indic doctrine and ritual structure under which a practitioner's relation to the divine is mediated through a single chosen deity-form (the *iṣṭa-devatā*) rather than through the divine in undifferentiated abstraction. The *iṣṭa-devatā* is typically received from the *guru* at *dīkṣā* (initiation) along with the deity's *mantra*, and becomes the focus of the practitioner's daily *sādhana*: *japa*, *dhyāna*, *pūjā*, *nāma-saṅkīrtana* are all conducted with reference to the *iṣṭa-devatā*. The doctrine functions across traditions: in Pāñcarātra Vaiṣṇavism, the *iṣṭa-devatā* is one of the *vibhava*-incarnations or one of the *vyūha*-forms; in Śaiva traditions, it is Śiva in one of his determinate forms; in Śākta traditions, it is the goddess in one of her *mūrtis* (Tripurā-Sundarī, Kālī, Durgā, etc.). The Vīraśaiva *iṣṭa-liṅga* is a special form of *iṣṭa-devatā* practice in which the chosen deity is Śiva-as-*liṅga* worn on the body. The Smārta tradition formalises *iṣṭa-devatā* in the *pañcāyatana-pūjā* structure, in which any of five deities (Viṣṇu, Śiva, Devī, Sūrya, Gaṇeśa) may be the central *iṣṭa-devatā* with the others supporting.

per_school.definition: The *iṣṭa-devatā* is typically a *vibhava*-incarnation (Rāma, Kṛṣṇa, Narasiṃha, etc.) or a *vyūha*-form; the *dīkṣā*-mantra fixes the practitioner's relation to that form, and the *pañca-kāla* discipline is conducted in reference to it.
The *iṣṭa-devatā* is Śiva-Bhairava in one of the determinate forms of the Bhairava-tantra canon; *dīkṣā* fixes the practitioner's relation through the source-tantra's mantra-cosmology.
The *iṣṭa-devatā* is the goddess in one of her determinate forms (Tripurā-Sundarī in Śrīvidyā, Kālī in Krama); the *śrī-vidyā* mantra and the *cakra*-cosmology fix the relation.

The *iṣṭa-devatā* takes the special form of the *iṣṭa-liṅga* — a personal *liṅga* worn on the body and worshipped throughout life as Śiva-as-such. The doctrine is that the *iṣṭa-liṅga* is identical with one's own essential consciousness as Śiva.

The *iṣṭa-devatā* may be any of five deities (Viṣṇu, Śiva, Devī, Sūrya, Gaṇeśa) in the standard Smārta *pañcāyatana-pūjā* structure; the chosen *iṣṭa* takes the central position and the other four are arranged around it.
The *iṣṭa-devatā* is Kṛṣṇa in one of the *līlā*-forms (typically the *ujjvala-rasa* form for the Gauḍīya tradition); the *iṣṭa* relation is the structural ground of *bhakti-rasa* development.

translator_note: 'Chosen deity' or 'personal deity' acceptable. The Western reader should not import the Christian-Protestant 'personal relation with God' frame uncritically — the *iṣṭa-devatā* relation is mediated through *guru*, *mantra*, *dīkṣā*, and ritual discipline, and is not a sentimental-individualist private religion. The choice of *iṣṭa* is conditioned by lineage, *guru*-transmission, and natural inclination (*adhikāra*) rather than by free individual selection from a deity-menu.
